title: Merck — HIV — Thesis
html:
HIV Thesis Tracker — Vinoth's POV

TA: HIV Treatment — Doravirine/Islatravir (IDVYNSO, Pifeltro, Delstrigo) Client: Merck Initiated: 2026-04-24 Last reviewed: 2026-04-24 Review cadence: Quarterly (next: July 2026)

How to use this document

Each thesis is a forward-looking bet — not a fact summary. The value is in being wrong and knowing why you were wrong. Update quarterly:

  • Did new evidence strengthen or weaken the thesis?
  • Should the thesis be revised, retired, or replaced?
  • What would you tell a Merck stakeholder if they asked your view on this?

Thesis 1: IDVYNSO's ceiling is defined by the "non-INSTI niche" vs "oral simplification platform" framing battle

The bet: If Merck positions IDVYNSO only as an INSTI-sparing option for patients with weight gain or tolerability concerns, the addressable market stays small (~10-15% of PLWH). If they successfully reframe it as "modern oral simplification — fewer drugs, no tenofovir, no INSTI, proven vs Biktarvy" — the TAM expands to all suppressed patients considering a switch, which is the majority of the ~1.2M PLWH on ART.

Why this matters for Vinoth: The ZoomRx SFE tracking (starting 2H 2026) will be the first external measurement of which framing Merck's field force is executing. If reps default to the familiar Pifeltro playbook (comorbidities, lipids, weight — niche patients), IDVYNSO underperforms. If they lead with the head-to-head Biktarvy data and simplification story, the uptake curve steepens. ZoomRx's message recall data will reveal which framing is winning in the field.

Evidence FOR (niche stays small):

  • Pifeltro's 7 years of niche positioning has trained the sales force to sell tolerability, not platform superiority
  • Biktarvy's 90% sampling penetration and DHHS rapid initiation recommendation create a gravitational pull that's hard to escape
  • IDVYNSO's initial label is switch-only — limits the narrative to existing patients, not new starts
  • HIV market is famously sticky; physicians don't switch stable patients without a compelling reason

Evidence AGAINST (platform framing can win):

  • CROI 2026 treatment-naive data gives Merck the efficacy story to go broad — non-inferior to Biktarvy in all comers
  • Non-INSTI + tenofovir-free + 2-drug resonates across multiple physician concerns simultaneously
  • Weight and metabolic concerns with INSTIs are growing in medical literature and patient advocacy
  • PASO DOBLE data (Dovato vs Biktarvy) already legitimized the "fewer drugs" framing — Merck can draft behind it

Confidence: Medium. This thesis resolves within 2-3 quarters based on early SFE data and prescribing patterns.

What to watch: First ZoomRx SFE wave (topline July 2026) — specifically, which IDVYNSO messages are being delivered and recalled. If reps lead with "non-inferior to Biktarvy" (efficacy/broad) vs "lipid profile and renal" (niche), the trajectory becomes clear.

Thesis 2: Gilead's SOV dominance is the structural barrier — not clinical data

The bet: Merck's #3 sales force position (28% SOV vs Gilead's 42%) means that even with superior clinical positioning, IDVYNSO will struggle to achieve adequate HCP mindshare. The constraint isn't the data — it's the physics of detailing frequency. Gilead reaches ~80% of HCPs monthly; Merck reaches ~65%. The 15-point reach gap compounds over quarters into a massive awareness and comfort gap.

Why this matters for Vinoth: If this thesis holds, the highest-value ZoomRx insight isn't "is the IDVYNSO message working?" — it's "is Merck reaching enough HCPs with the IDVYNSO message?" The SFE study design (N=90 per wave, tracking vs Dovato and Biktarvy) will capture relative reach and frequency. Vinoth should push for a reach/frequency deep-dive in the Wave 1 report.

Evidence FOR:

  • ZoomRx PET data (Aug-Dec 2025): Merck consistently 3rd on reach, frequency, and SOV across both ID and PCP segments
  • Merck's NP/PA reach is notably lower than competitors — this segment is growing in HIV management
  • Biktarvy's 90% sampling penetration creates a tangible presence (physical product in office) that advertising can't match
  • Gilead's NPP reach also leads Merck's, creating a multi-channel advantage

Evidence AGAINST:

  • IDVYNSO launch is a fresh start — Merck can reallocate field force resources from Pifeltro/Delstrigo (confirmed: reps will ONLY promote DOR/ISL)
  • A new product launch creates natural physician curiosity — the "what's new?" effect can temporarily override SOV dynamics
  • Merck's rep interaction quality is already competitive (86% visual aid usage, improving rep performance scores, 16.3 min avg duration — longest in market)
  • Sampling study confirms HCPs prefer rep delivery — if Merck samples IDVYNSO aggressively at launch, it can close the presence gap faster

Confidence: High. SOV gaps don't close in one quarter. But the IDVYNSO launch creates a window of opportunity.

What to watch: Wave 1 SFE data — reach and frequency for DOR/ISL vs competitors. If Merck's reach jumps >5 points from current Pifeltro levels, the launch energy is real.

Thesis 3: The oral simplification trend will outrun the injectable trend for the next 3-5 years

The bet: Despite the excitement around long-acting injectables (Cabenuva monthly, lenacapavir twice-yearly), the majority of PLWH will remain on daily oral regimens through 2030. The injectable conversation is louder than the injectable reality — most patients are well-controlled on oral pills, comfortable with the routine, and reluctant to switch to injections requiring clinic visits. IDVYNSO's oral simplification play (2-drug, non-INSTI, tenofovir-free) addresses the larger, quieter demand.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This thesis determines whether Merck's oral-only HIV strategy is well-timed or structurally disadvantaged. If injectables accelerate faster than expected, IDVYNSO's competitive window shrinks. If oral remains dominant, Merck has positioned itself as the best oral simplification option without INSTI baggage.

Evidence FOR:

  • Cabenuva uptake, while growing, is projected at ~$2.5-3B by 2026 — a fraction of the oral market
  • ZoomRx sampling study: majority of HCPs still prefer oral regimens for their patients; injection logistics (scheduling, clinic visits, injection site reactions) are real barriers
  • Lenacapavir for treatment is still in trials — broad treatment approval is 2-3 years away
  • Merck's own pipeline (islatravir + lenacapavir once-weekly oral) hedges the injectable bet — Merck is playing both sides

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Lenacapavir's PrEP success (PURPOSE 1 & 2 trials) creates massive momentum for the molecule; treatment approval is a matter of when, not if
  • Patient advocacy groups strongly support long-acting options for adherence-challenged populations
  • Gilead's commercial machine behind lenacapavir will be formidable if/when it gets broad treatment approval
  • The "fewer pills" argument could extend to "no pills" — injectables are the logical endpoint of simplification

Confidence: Medium-high for 3-year horizon. By 2030, injectables likely capture 20-30% share, but oral remains majority.

What to watch: Lenacapavir treatment trial readouts. Cabenuva quarterly revenue growth rate. ISLEND trial (Merck's own oral weekly islatravir + lenacapavir) — if this succeeds, Merck pivots to weekly oral and IDVYNSO becomes a bridge product.

Thesis 4: Merck's sampling and rep delivery strategy is the underappreciated launch lever

The bet: In a market where 90% of HCPs receive Biktarvy samples via rep delivery, and where HCPs report 17% higher preference for rep-delivered samples among Merck's target list, the physical act of getting IDVYNSO samples into physician offices will matter more than messaging or clinical data in the first 6-12 months of launch. Sampling creates trial, trial creates familiarity, familiarity creates comfort.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx's July 2025 sampling preference study is the only external data on HIV sampling dynamics. This positions ZoomRx as the authority on sampling strategy — and gives Vinoth a unique talking point with Merck: "We know how HCPs want to receive your new product." The 2026 SFE study should include sampling-related questions for DOR/ISL.

Evidence FOR:

  • ZoomRx sampling study: 55% prefer rep delivery, 64% among Merck's target list HCPs
  • Biktarvy's 90% sampling penetration correlates with market dominance — sampling IS adoption in HIV
  • "Patients can start treatment right away" is the #1 benefit of rep delivery (60% of HCPs)
  • Sales rep delivery satisfaction is 11 points higher than mail delivery for HIV treatment samples (86% vs 75%)

Evidence AGAINST:

  • IDVYNSO is a switch product — most patients are already on a regimen. Samples matter less for switch than for new starts
  • Merck's smaller field force may not be able to match Gilead's sampling frequency
  • Mail delivery preference is growing (45%), especially among PCPs — rep-only strategy may miss a segment
  • Sampling alone doesn't overcome clinical inertia if physicians aren't convinced of clinical differentiation

Confidence: Medium. Sampling is necessary but not sufficient. It's the "table stakes" thesis.

What to watch: Merck's sampling strategy post-launch (rep-only vs hybrid). DOR/ISL sampling penetration in Wave 1 SFE data (if tracked). Compare to Biktarvy's 90% benchmark.

Thesis 5: Islatravir's clinical history creates a perception risk that Merck must actively manage

The bet: Islatravir's earlier development setback — dose-dependent CD4+ T-cell count declines observed in the PrEP program at higher doses, leading to clinical holds and program restructuring — has left a residual perception risk among infectious disease physicians. Even though the approved IDVYNSO dose (0.25mg) showed no clinically meaningful CD4 effect, the "islatravir had safety issues" narrative lives in the ID community. If Merck doesn't proactively address this in field force training and MSL communications, it becomes an unspoken objection that suppresses adoption.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This is the kind of nuanced perception barrier that ZoomRx's SFE study can detect. If Wave 1 shows unexpectedly low prescribing intent despite strong message recall, the islatravir perception overhang may be the explanation. Vinoth should recommend including a specific perception question about islatravir safety confidence in the study design.

Evidence FOR:

  • IDs at academic centers followed the islatravir development closely; the clinical hold was widely discussed
  • "Novel MOA" can be a double-edged sword — excitement for innovation vs caution about unknowns
  • Merck's competitors will subtly reference the CD4 history in competitive positioning
  • PCPs, who know less about the development history, may actually adopt faster than IDs (ironic reversal)

Evidence AGAINST:

  • The approved dose (0.25mg) is 10-40x lower than the doses that caused CD4 effects in PrEP
  • Phase 3 data at CROI 2026 showed comparable safety to Biktarvy at Week 48 and 96
  • FDA approved the product — the ultimate safety validation
  • Physicians routinely prescribe drugs with class-level concerns if the data for the specific dose is clean

Confidence: Medium. This is a "soft" barrier — hard to measure, but real in its effect on adoption speed.

What to watch: Wave 1 SFE — prescribing intent for DOR/ISL vs competitors. Unaided objection capture. MSL engagement requests (high MSL request rate may signal physician uncertainty).

Summary: Net Assessment

Thesis Confidence Resolves By
1. Niche vs platform framing Medium Q4 2026 (SFE Wave 1)
2. Gilead SOV dominance High Q1 2027 (2 SFE waves)
3. Oral > injectable for 3-5 years Medium-high 2027-2028
4. Sampling as launch lever Medium Q4 2026 (launch tracking)
5. Islatravir perception risk Medium Q4 2026 (SFE Wave 1)

Vinoth's overall read: Merck's IDVYNSO is a legitimate entrant with real clinical differentiation — the first time Merck has had a product that can go head-to-head with Biktarvy on data. The question is not "is the product good enough?" — it is. The question is "can Merck's commercial infrastructure compete with Gilead's?" That's what every ZoomRx study in this space should be measuring.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
